title: Recovery
subtitle: Foam rolling + mobility
coaching_note: This is training. Not optional, not a reward - a session. Whatever areas feel tight or achy from your other sessions need this as much as the strength work does. Slow down, breathe into tight spots.

phases.name: Foam Rolling
phases.coaching_note: Roll slowly. When you find a tender spot, stop and hold 5-10 seconds. Don't just roll fast - that does nothing.

exercises.name: Quad Roll
exercises.form_cue: Face down, roller under quads. Use forearms to control pressure. Rotate leg inward and outward to hit different angles.
exercises.why: Tight quads pull the pelvis forward and load the lower back. Your run training will make this worse without maintenance.

exercises.name: IT Band / Outer Hip Roll
exercises.form_cue: Side-lying, roller on outer thigh. Stack top foot on floor for pressure control. Roll from hip to just above knee.
exercises.why: IT band tightness contributes to knee pain on runs. Work both sides evenly, but spend extra time wherever you feel the most tension.

exercises.name: Glute / Piriformis Roll
exercises.form_cue: Sit on roller, cross ankle over opposite knee. Lean into the crossed-leg side. This targets the piriformis directly.
exercises.why: The glutes and piriformis tighten up fast from squatting, running, and single-leg work. This is often the most tender spot, so give it real time.

exercises.name: Upper Back / Thoracic Roll
exercises.form_cue: Roller across upper back (T4-T8 range, not neck or lower back). Arms crossed over chest or hands behind head. Extend over the roller gently. Work segment by segment.
exercises.why: Thoracic restriction forces the lower back to compensate for movement it shouldn't be doing. Open this up, especially after desk time or overhead sport work.

exercises.name: Calf Roll
exercises.form_cue: Roller under calf. Stack legs for more pressure. Rotate foot inward and outward. Flex and point the ankle.
exercises.why: Tight calves reduce ankle dorsiflexion which loads the knee and hip on runs. Post-run essential.

phases.name: Stretching
phases.coaching_note: Hold each stretch at least 45-60 seconds. Don't bounce. Breathe into the stretch - exhale to go deeper.

exercises.name: Hip Flexor Stretch (kneeling lunge)
exercises.form_cue: Back knee on floor, front foot flat. Drive hips forward gently. Keep torso upright - don't lean forward.
exercises.why: Tight hip flexors tilt the pelvis and compress the lower back. Critical for running posture.

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose (QL / glute stretch)
exercises.form_cue: Front shin across mat, back leg extended. Fold forward over the front leg. If hips don't reach the floor, put a folded towel under the hip.
exercises.why: Directly targets the glute and QL on each side. Spend extra time on whichever side feels tighter.

exercises.name: Supine Hamstring Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Lying on back. Loop pilates band around foot. Straighten leg toward ceiling. Keep lower back flat on floor. Don't yank.
exercises.why: Tight hamstrings limit hip flexion in running stride and load the lower back.

exercises.name: Thoracic Rotation
exercises.form_cue: Lying on side, knees stacked at 90 degrees. Top arm sweeps across to the other side, following with the eye and head. Let the chest open. Hold 2 seconds at the end.
exercises.why: Directly addresses thoracic mobility restriction, which is common for anyone doing overhead sport or desk work and often ends up loading the lower back instead.

exercises.name: Child's Pose
exercises.form_cue: Knees wide, arms extended forward, forehead to floor. Breathe into the lower back. Let gravity do the work.
exercises.why: Decompresses the lumbar spine. Good closer for any session where the lower back worked hard.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Stretch
exercises.form_cue: Heel on floor, toes up on wall. Keep knee straight for gastrocnemius, slight bend for soleus. Feel the difference.
exercises.why: Post-run essential. Prevents plantar fasciitis and Achilles issues as run volume builds.

progression_notes: If a hip is flaring, do an extra set of pigeon pose on that side and add a 90-90 hip stretch. If this session follows an overhead sport or upper-body-heavy training, prioritize thoracic rotation and the glute roll.
